Publish identity as DNS TXT record at _locd.<domain> with fields: v=locd1, k=ed25519, p=<pubkey>, t=<timestamp>, optional exp=<expiry>, optional rev=<revocation-url>
Publish identity to cooperative namespace at <username>._locd.id.locd.net for users without their own domain
Perform identity lookup by resolving DNS TXT record at _locd.<domain> and parsing all record fields
Validate DNSSEC chain on every identity lookup and reject records that fail DNSSEC validation
Perform identity lookups via DNS-over-HTTPS (RFC 8484) to prevent network observers from seeing which identities are being verified
Support multiple independent identities per user, each with its own Master Key, DNS record, delegation scope, and revocation scope
Enforce TTL recommendations: 300s for active identity records, 300s for cooperative namespace records, 60s for revoked/expired identities
Handle identity expiry semantics: reject identities past their exp timestamp, treat absent exp as non-expiring
Validate identity record field format: required fields (v, k, p, t) must be present and well-formed; optional fields (exp, rev) validated when present
Cache resolved identity records respecting DNS TTL, re-query on TTL expiry, never cache past delegation token expires_at
acceptance_criteria: Given a device with a hardware secure enclave, when identity creation is invoked, then an Ed25519 key pair is generated inside the secure enclave and the private key never leaves the enclave boundary
Given a generated Ed25519 public key of 32 bytes, when encoded as base64url without padding, then the resulting identity string is exactly 43 characters
Given a valid identity, when publishing to DNS, then a TXT record is created at _locd.<domain> containing v=locd1; k=ed25519; p=<base64url-pubkey>; t=<unix-timestamp> with all required fields present
Given a cooperative namespace user 'alice', when publishing identity, then the TXT record is created at alice._locd.id.locd.net with identical field format
Given a domain with a valid _locd TXT record and valid DNSSEC chain, when performing identity lookup, then the public key is correctly extracted and returned
Given a domain with a _locd TXT record but invalid or missing DNSSEC chain, when performing identity lookup, then the lookup is rejected with reason code dnssec_invalid
Given a domain with no _locd TXT record, when performing identity lookup, then the lookup returns identity_not_found reason code
Given an identity record with exp field set to a past Unix timestamp, when performing identity lookup, then the lookup is rejected with reason code identity_expired
Given an identity record with no exp field, when performing identity lookup, then the identity is treated as non-expiring and lookup succeeds
Given an identity record with v field not equal to locd1, when parsing the record, then the record is rejected as an unsupported protocol version
Given an identity record with k field not equal to ed25519, when parsing the record, then the record is rejected as an unsupported key algorithm
Given an identity record with p field that is not valid base64url or does not decode to exactly 32 bytes, when parsing the record, then the record is rejected as malformed
Given an identity record with t field that is not a valid Unix timestamp, when parsing the record, then the record is rejected as malformed
Given an identity record with a rev field, when parsed, then the revocation endpoint URL is extracted and stored for later revocation checks
Given DoH is enabled, when performing identity lookups, then all DNS queries are sent over HTTPS (RFC 8484) and no plaintext DNS queries are emitted
Given a resolved identity record with TTL of 300 seconds, when the same identity is looked up within 300 seconds, then the cached result is returned without a new DNS query
Given a cached identity record whose TTL has expired, when the identity is looked up again, then a fresh DNS query is performed
Given a user with two independent identities (personal and professional), when each identity is looked up, then each returns its own distinct public key and metadata
Given signing operations on the Master Key, when invoked, then biometric authentication or device PIN is required before the signing operation proceeds
Given a revoked or expired identity record, when TTL is configured, then the TTL is set to 60 seconds to accelerate propagation of the revoked state
technical_spec: Ed25519 key generation must use RFC 8032 compliant implementation within the platform's hardware secure enclave (iOS Secure Enclave API, Android Keystore with StrongBox attestation, or TPM 2.0 on desktop)

Private key material must never be exported from the secure enclave; all signing operations must be performed via the enclave's signing API (e.g., SecKeyCreateSignature on iOS, Signature.sign() with AndroidKeyStore provider)
Base64url encoding must follow RFC 4648 Section 5 with no padding characters, producing exactly 43 characters from a 32-byte Ed25519 public key
DNS TXT record parsing must handle semicolon-delimited key=value pairs with optional whitespace around delimiters, and must be tolerant of field ordering
DNS TXT records exceeding 255 bytes must be handled per RFC 7208 Section 3.3 by concatenating multiple character-strings within a single TXT record
DNSSEC validation must verify the full chain of trust from the root zone to the _locd record, using DS/DNSKEY/RRSIG records per RFC 4033-4035
DNS-over-HTTPS implementation must conform to RFC 8484, using application/dns-message content type and supporting both GET and POST methods
DoH resolver selection should be configurable by the user, defaulting to well-known public resolvers (e.g., Cloudflare 1.1.1.1, Google 8.8.8.8) but allowing self-hosted resolvers
Identity record caching must store the full parsed record along with its DNS TTL, and must evict entries when TTL expires or when a revocation is detected for that identity
Cooperative namespace records at <username>._locd.id.locd.net must follow the same TXT record format and validation rules as custom domain records
Cooperative namespace registration must enforce username uniqueness, character restrictions (alphanumeric plus hyphens, 3-63 characters), and must not allow reserved names (e.g., www, admin, api)
Multiple identity support requires complete isolation: each identity has its own Master Key, DNS record location, delegation token set, and revocation scope with no cross-contamination
Timestamp validation must reject records where t is more than 60 seconds in the future relative to the verifier's clock to prevent pre-dated records while allowing minor clock skew
The rev field URL must be validated as a well-formed HTTPS URL; HTTP-only URLs must be rejected to prevent revocation list tampering
Error handling must return structured reason codes (dns_lookup_failed, dnssec_invalid, identity_not_found, identity_expired) as defined in Section 7.4 of the protocol spec
Identity record publication must support both creating new records and updating existing records (for key rotation or metadata changes) via DNS update mechanisms or registrar API
All cryptographic operations must use constant-time comparison functions to prevent timing side-channel attacks when verifying signatures or comparing public keys
Identity Layer.api_contract: Core interfaces: (1) createIdentity() -> { publicKey: bytes(32), identityString: string(43), createdAt: uint } - generates Ed25519 key pair in secure enclave, returns public component as base64url. (2) publishIdentity(domain: string, publicKey: bytes, options: { exp?: uint, rev?: string }) -> { record: string, location: string } - formats and publishes DNS TXT record at _locd.<domain>. (3) lookupIdentity(domain: string, options: { useDoH?: bool }) -> { publicKey: bytes, publishedAt: uint, expiresAt?: uint, revocationUrl?: string, dnssecValid: bool } | { error: ReasonCode } - resolves and validates identity record with DNSSEC verification. (4) validateRecord(txtRecord: string) -> { valid: bool, fields: ParsedRecord, errors?: string[] } - parses and validates TXT record field format. (5) cacheGet(domain: string) -> CachedIdentity | null - retrieves cached identity if TTL not expired. (6) cacheSet(domain: string, identity: ResolvedIdentity, ttl: uint) -> void - stores resolved identity with TTL. (7) registerCooperativeNamespace(username: string, publicKey: bytes) -> { subdomain: string, record: string } - registers username at id.locd.net cooperative namespace. ReasonCode enum: dns_lookup_failed | dnssec_invalid | identity_not_found | identity_expired | version_unsupported | algorithm_unsupported | malformed_record.
dependencies: Hardware secure enclave access layer (iOS Secure Enclave, Android StrongBox/Keystore, TPM 2.0) for Ed25519 key generation and signing
Ed25519 cryptographic library compliant with RFC 8032 (e.g., libsodium, ring, or platform-native APIs)
DNS resolver library with DNSSEC validation support (e.g., trust-dns, getdns, or unbound bindings)
DNS-over-HTTPS client implementation conforming to RFC 8484
Base64url encoding/decoding library conforming to RFC 4648 Section 5
DNS record management API or registrar integration for publishing TXT records
Cooperative namespace registration service at id.locd.net (external service dependency)
Revocation Layer module for checking rev endpoint during identity validation
Platform biometric authentication APIs (TouchID/FaceID on iOS, BiometricPrompt on Android) for gating Master Key signing operations

Delegation Layer.summary: The Delegation Layer implements the creation, signing, validation, and lifecycle management of CBOR-encoded delegation tokens that authorize Device Keys to act on behalf of a Master Key with scoped, time-limited, revocable authority. Tokens are signed as COSE Sign1 structures using EdDSA (Ed25519) and encode 11 fields covering identity binding, temporal constraints, service/action scoping, usage limits, device attestation, and sub-delegation control. The module enforces strict constraints including a maximum 30-day expiry window (default 24 hours), domain pattern matching for permitted services, action scope validation, usage counting, and sub-delegation chain validation with a maximum depth of 3. It also supports automatic token renewal when the Master Key device is reachable on the mesh.
features: Delegation token creation with all 11 CBOR map fields: type, delegator, delegate, issued_at, expires_at, delegation_id, services, actions, max_uses, attestation, can_sub_delegate
COSE Sign1 wrapping and Ed25519 signing of delegation tokens by the Master Key
Delegation token deserialization from CBOR and COSE Sign1 signature verification against a known Master Key public key
Temporal constraint enforcement: expires_at must not exceed 30 days from issued_at, default expiry of 24 hours, reject tokens with issued_at more than 60 seconds in the future
Service scope validation using domain pattern matching against the services array in the delegation token
Action scope validation against the actions array in the delegation token
Usage counter tracking and enforcement of max_uses limit per delegation token
Sub-delegation token creation by delegates with can_sub_delegate=true, producing chained tokens
Sub-delegation chain validation: chain depth max 3, scope must not expand beyond parent, expiry must not exceed parent expires_at
Auto-renewal flow: clients request renewed tokens before expiry when the Master Key device is reachable
Delegation ID generation using UUID v4 for unique identification and revocation targeting
Optional TPM 2.0 attestation quote inclusion and validation in delegation tokens
Serialization of delegation tokens to wire-ready CBOR bytes for inclusion in RESPONSE messages
Delegation chain serialization: ordered root-first array of COSE Sign1 tokens for sub-delegated identities
acceptance_criteria: A delegation token created with all required fields (type, delegator, delegate, issued_at, expires_at, delegation_id) serializes to valid CBOR and deserializes back to identical values
The type field in every created token is exactly the string 'locd-delegation-v1'
The delegator field contains exactly the 32-byte Ed25519 public key of the Master Key that signs the token
The delegate field contains exactly the 32-byte Ed25519 public key of the authorized Device Key
A token wrapped in COSE Sign1 with algorithm header -8 (EdDSA) verifies successfully against the Master Key's public key
A token with a tampered payload byte fails COSE Sign1 signature verification
A token with a forged signature (valid format but wrong key) fails signature verification
Creating a token with expires_at more than 30 days (2,592,000 seconds) after issued_at is rejected with an error
Creating a token with expires_at exactly 30 days after issued_at succeeds
When no explicit expires_at is provided, the default expiry is set to 24 hours (86,400 seconds) after issued_at
A token with issued_at more than 60 seconds in the future relative to the verifier's clock is rejected during validation
A token with issued_at exactly 60 seconds in the future relative to the verifier's clock is accepted during validation
A token with expires_at in the past relative to the verifier's clock is rejected as expired during validation
A token with services=['*.example.com'] matches 'api.example.com' and 'dashboard.example.com' but does not match 'api.other.com'
A token with services=['api.example.com'] matches exactly 'api.example.com' and does not match 'other.example.com'
A token with an empty services array or absent services field permits all services during scope validation
A token with actions=['read','write'] permits 'read' and 'write' but rejects 'admin' during action scope validation
A token with an empty actions array or absent actions field permits all actions during scope validation
A token with max_uses=5 allows exactly 5 successful verifications and rejects the 6th attempt
A token with max_uses=0 or absent max_uses field allows unlimited verifications
A token with max_uses=1 allows exactly one verification and rejects all subsequent attempts
A delegate with can_sub_delegate=true can create a valid sub-delegation token signed by the delegate's Device Key
A delegate with can_sub_delegate=false or absent can_sub_delegate is rejected when attempting to create a sub-delegation token
A sub-delegated token that expands services scope beyond the parent delegation (e.g., parent allows ['api.example.com'] but sub-delegation allows ['*.example.com']) is rejected
A sub-delegated token that adds actions not present in the parent delegation is rejected
A sub-delegated token with expires_at later than the parent delegation's expires_at is rejected
A sub-delegation chain of depth 3 (Master -> Device A -> Device B -> Device C) validates successfully
A sub-delegation chain of depth 4 (Master -> A -> B -> C -> D) is rejected with a chain_too_deep error
The delegation chain in a RESPONSE message is ordered root-first: the Master Key delegation is at index 0
Each delegation_id generated is a valid UUID v4 string
No two delegation tokens created in sequence produce the same delegation_id
Auto-renewal requests a new token when the current token is within 10% of its remaining lifetime or within 1 hour of expiry, whichever is larger
Auto-renewal fails gracefully when the Master Key device is unreachable, allowing the current token to expire naturally without error
A renewed token has a new delegation_id, new issued_at, and new expires_at but preserves the same delegate key, services, actions, and scope constraints
When a TPM attestation quote is provided in field 10, it is included in the CBOR payload and preserved through serialization/deserialization
A fully populated token with all 11 fields serializes to CBOR and the COSE Sign1 envelope produces valid bytes that can be transmitted in the wire format
technical_spec: Use CBOR (RFC 8949) for token payload encoding with integer map keys (1-11) as specified in the delegation token structure
Use COSE Sign1 (RFC 9052) for the signing envelope with protected header containing algorithm identifier -8 (EdDSA)
Use Ed25519 (RFC 8032) for all delegation token signatures; signing must be performed within the secure enclave/TPM boundary
Public keys are 32-byte raw Ed25519 public keys stored as CBOR byte strings (not base64-encoded within the token)
Delegation IDs must be UUID v4 (RFC 4122) generated with a cryptographically secure random number generator
Service domain patterns support exact match ('api.example.com') and wildcard prefix match ('*.example.com') where * matches any single subdomain label
Action strings are case-sensitive exact matches compared against the actions array in the delegation token
Max-uses tracking requires persistent per-delegation-id counter storage on the verifier side, atomically incremented on each successful verification

Sub-delegation validation must verify the complete chain: each link's signature, each link's scope is a subset of its parent, each link's expiry does not exceed its parent, and total chain depth does not exceed 3
Clock skew tolerance of 60 seconds applies to issued_at validation; timestamps are Unix epoch seconds as unsigned integers
Auto-renewal should be triggered proactively by the client library, not by the verifier; the client monitors token expiry and contacts the Master Key device over the mesh
Token serialization must produce deterministic CBOR output (canonical CBOR per RFC 8949 Section 4.2) to ensure consistent signature verification
The COSE Sign1 external_aad (additional authenticated data) must be empty bytes as per the specification
For sub-delegation chains, the RESPONSE message carries an array of COSE Sign1 byte strings ordered from root delegation to leaf delegation
Memory handling: delegation token plaintext and intermediate signing material must be zeroed after use to prevent memory disclosure
Error reporting must use the reason codes defined in Section 7.4: delegation_invalid, delegation_expired, delegation_revoked, scope_violation, chain_too_deep, attestation_failed
Delegation Layer.api_contract: Core interfaces: (1) create_delegation(master_key_signer, delegate_pubkey, options) -> CoseSigned<DelegationToken> - creates and signs a new delegation token with specified constraints; options include services, actions, max_uses, expires_in, attestation, can_sub_delegate. (2) verify_delegation(token_bytes, master_pubkey, context) -> VerificationResult - deserializes COSE Sign1, verifies signature against master_pubkey, checks all temporal constraints, scope constraints against context.service and context.action, revocation status, and returns verified token or error with reason code. (3) create_sub_delegation(parent_chain, delegate_signer, sub_delegate_pubkey, options) -> CoseSigned<DelegationToken>[] - creates a sub-delegation token signed by the delegate, validates scope/expiry/depth constraints against parent chain, returns the extended chain. (4) verify_chain(chain_bytes[], master_pubkey, context) -> VerificationResult - verifies a complete delegation chain from root to leaf. (5) serialize_token(token) -> bytes - canonical CBOR serialization. (6) deserialize_token(bytes) -> DelegationToken - CBOR deserialization with validation. (7) track_usage(delegation_id) -> UsageResult - atomically increments and checks max_uses counter. (8) request_renewal(current_token, master_device_endpoint) -> CoseSigned<DelegationToken> | RenewalError - attempts to obtain a renewed delegation token from the Master Key device.
dependencies: CBOR library compliant with RFC 8949 including canonical/deterministic encoding support
COSE library compliant with RFC 9052 supporting Sign1 with EdDSA algorithm identifier -8
Ed25519 cryptographic library compliant with RFC 8032 with secure enclave/TPM integration for signing operations
UUID v4 generation library with cryptographically secure random source
Secure enclave / TPM 2.0 interface for Master Key signing operations (platform-specific: iOS Secure Enclave, Android StrongBox, TPM 2.0)
Revocation Layer module for checking delegation_id revocation status during verification
Mesh Connectivity Layer for auto-renewal communication with the Master Key device
Persistent storage backend for max_uses counter tracking per delegation_id on verifier nodes
Argon2id library for any passphrase-derived key operations related to recovery (indirect dependency via Recovery module)

Verification Layer.summary: The Verification Layer implements the core challenge-response authentication protocol for Loc'd, enabling cryptographic identity verification between Claimants and Verifiers. It processes a 4-message CBOR-encoded flow (HELLO, CHALLENGE, RESPONSE, RESULT) where a Claimant presents its identity domain and Device Key, receives a cryptographic challenge, signs it with its hardware-bound Device Key, and submits the signature alongside a COSE Sign1 delegation token chain for full verification. The Verifier performs DNS-based identity resolution with DNSSEC validation, validates delegation token signatures against the published Master Key, enforces scope constraints (services, actions, expiry, revocation, max uses), verifies the Ed25519 response signature against the challenge nonce and timestamp, and returns a structured result with one of 14 reason codes. The layer supports mutual authentication where the Verifier proves its identity back to the Claimant, and implements intelligent public key caching bounded by DNS TTL and delegation token expiry.
features: HELLO message processing: accept identity domain (e.g., lane.id.locd.net) and Claimant Device Key public key, validate CBOR structure, initiate DNS identity resolution
DNS identity resolution: query _locd.<domain> TXT record, parse v=locd1 format, extract Ed25519 public key, verify DNSSEC chain, reject on DNSSEC validation failure
CHALLENGE generation: produce 32-byte cryptographically random nonce, attach Unix timestamp, include Verifier's own identity domain for binding, CBOR-encode and transmit
RESPONSE processing: accept Ed25519 signature over (nonce || timestamp || verifier_domain), accept COSE Sign1 delegation token, accept optional sub-delegation chain ordered root-first
Delegation token verification: decode COSE Sign1 envelope, verify Ed25519 signature by published Master Key, validate CBOR payload structure matches locd-delegation-v1 schema

Delegation expiry enforcement: reject tokens where expires_at is in the past, reject tokens where expires_at exceeds 30 days from issued_at, reject tokens where issued_at is more than 60 seconds in the future

Delegation revocation checking: query _locd-revoke.<domain> DNS TXT record for revoked delegation IDs, query supplementary revocation list at rev URL from identity record, reject if delegation_id appears in either source

Service scope enforcement: validate that the Verifier's domain matches at least one entry in the delegation token's services array (domain pattern matching), pass if services field is absent or empty (all services permitted)
Action scope enforcement: validate requested action against the delegation token's actions array, pass if actions field is absent or empty (all actions permitted)
Max uses tracking: track per-delegation-ID usage count, reject when max_uses (if non-zero) is exceeded

Response signature verification: verify Ed25519 signature over concatenation of (nonce || timestamp || verifier_domain) using the delegate public key from the delegation token, confirm nonce matches issued challenge, confirm timestamp within 60-second tolerance

Sub-delegation chain verification: validate each link in the chain is signed by the delegator of the next level, verify scope does not expand beyond parent delegation, verify expiry does not exceed parent's expires_at, enforce maximum chain depth of 3
RESULT message generation: produce CBOR-encoded result with verified boolean, reason code string, and Verifier's WireGuard public key (on success)

All 14 reason codes: ok, dns_lookup_failed, dnssec_invalid, identity_not_found, identity_expired, delegation_invalid, delegation_expired, delegation_revoked, scope_violation, nonce_mismatch, timestamp_skew, chain_too_deep, attestation_failed, version_unsupported (protocol negotiation)
Mutual authentication: after Claimant verification succeeds, Verifier optionally proves its own identity using the same protocol in reverse direction

Public key caching: cache resolved identity public keys for DNS TTL duration (default 300s), never cache past delegation token expires_at, re-query on TTL expiry, serve cached on DNS unavailability (degraded mode)
Transport framing: length-prefixed frames with 4-byte big-endian u32 length header followed by CBOR payload, transport-agnostic (TCP, WebSocket, HTTP/2, HTTP/3)
Protocol version negotiation: validate v=locd1 in HELLO, respond with version_unsupported and supported versions list on mismatch
Graceful degradation: if DNS is unreachable, use cached identity (if within TTL), if delegation is near expiry warn but continue, never fail silently
acceptance_criteria: Given a valid HELLO message with a known identity domain and Device Key, when the Verifier processes it, then it resolves the _locd.<domain> DNS TXT record and extracts the Ed25519 public key within 2 seconds
Given a HELLO message referencing a domain without a _locd TXT record, when the Verifier processes it, then it returns a RESULT with verified=false and reason='identity_not_found'
Given a DNS response that fails DNSSEC validation, when the Verifier checks the identity record, then it returns a RESULT with verified=false and reason='dnssec_invalid'

Given a valid CHALLENGE is generated, when inspected, then it contains exactly 32 bytes of cryptographically random nonce, a Unix timestamp within 1 second of current time, and the Verifier's identity domain

Given a valid RESPONSE with correct Ed25519 signature and valid delegation token, when the Verifier processes all checks, then it returns a RESULT with verified=true, reason='ok', and includes the Verifier's WireGuard public key
Given a RESPONSE where the delegation token's Ed25519 signature does not match the Master Key from DNS, when the Verifier checks delegation validity, then it returns reason='delegation_invalid'
Given a RESPONSE where the delegation token's expires_at is in the past, when the Verifier checks delegation validity, then it returns reason='delegation_expired'
Given a RESPONSE where the delegation token's issued_at is more than 60 seconds in the future, when the Verifier checks delegation validity, then it returns reason='delegation_expired'
Given a RESPONSE where the delegation token's expires_at exceeds 30 days from its issued_at, when the Verifier checks delegation validity, then it returns reason='delegation_invalid'
Given a delegation ID that appears in the _locd-revoke.<domain> DNS TXT record, when the Verifier checks revocation, then it returns reason='delegation_revoked'
Given a delegation ID that appears in the supplementary revocation list at the rev URL, when the Verifier checks revocation, then it returns reason='delegation_revoked'
Given a delegation token with services=['api.example.com'] and the Verifier's domain is 'other.example.com', when scope is checked, then it returns reason='scope_violation'
Given a delegation token with services=['*.example.com'] and the Verifier's domain is 'api.example.com', when scope is checked, then verification passes the service scope check
Given a delegation token with actions=['read'] and the requested action is 'write', when scope is checked, then it returns reason='scope_violation'
Given a delegation token with an absent services field, when scope is checked, then all services are permitted and the check passes
Given a delegation token with max_uses=5 and 5 prior uses recorded, when the Verifier checks usage count, then it returns reason='scope_violation'
Given a RESPONSE where the Ed25519 signature does not match the challenge nonce, when signature verification runs, then it returns reason='nonce_mismatch'
Given a RESPONSE where the timestamp differs by more than 60 seconds from the challenge timestamp, when timestamp tolerance is checked, then it returns reason='timestamp_skew'
Given a sub-delegation chain of depth 4, when chain verification runs, then it returns reason='chain_too_deep'
Given a sub-delegation chain where a child token's services scope is broader than its parent's, when chain verification runs, then it returns reason='scope_violation'
Given a sub-delegation chain where a child token's expires_at exceeds its parent's expires_at, when chain verification runs, then it returns reason='delegation_invalid'
Given a previously resolved public key within DNS TTL, when the same identity is verified again, then the cached key is used without a new DNS query
Given a cached public key whose DNS TTL has expired, when the same identity is verified, then a fresh DNS query is performed
Given a cached public key and the delegation token's expires_at is before the DNS TTL expiry, when caching decisions are made, then the cache entry expires at the delegation token's expires_at
Given DNS is unreachable but a valid cached key exists within TTL, when verification is attempted, then the cached key is used and verification proceeds in degraded mode
Given DNS is unreachable and no cached key exists, when verification is attempted, then it returns reason='dns_lookup_failed'

Given mutual authentication is requested, when the initial Claimant verification succeeds, then the Verifier performs the same challenge-response protocol in reverse to prove its identity to the Claimant
Given any protocol message, when it is serialized, then it is valid CBOR that can be decoded by any RFC 8949 compliant parser
Given a wire frame, when it is received, then it has a 4-byte big-endian u32 length prefix followed by exactly that many bytes of CBOR payload
Given a HELLO with an unsupported protocol version, when processed, then the Verifier returns reason='version_unsupported' with a list of supported versions

All 14 reason codes (ok, dns_lookup_failed, dnssec_invalid, identity_not_found, identity_expired, delegation_invalid, delegation_expired, delegation_revoked, scope_violation, nonce_mismatch, timestamp_skew, chain_too_deep, attestation_failed, version_unsupported) are returned in their correct error scenarios
technical_spec: CBOR encoding/decoding: use RFC 8949 compliant library for all message serialization; HELLO uses CBOR map keys {1: type, 2: domain, 3: device_key_bytes}; CHALLENGE uses {1: type, 2: nonce_bytes, 3: timestamp_uint, 4: verifier_domain}; RESPONSE uses {1: type, 2: signature_bytes, 3: delegation_cose_bytes, 4: chain_array}; RESULT uses {1: type, 2: verified_bool, 3: reason_text, 4: wireguard_key_bytes}

COSE Sign1 delegation token parsing: decode outer COSE_Sign1 structure per RFC 9052 with protected header {1: -8} (EdDSA algorithm), extract CBOR payload containing delegation map with keys 1-11, verify Ed25519 signature over Sig_structure1 = ['Signature1', protected, external_aad='', payload]

Ed25519 signature verification: use RFC 8032 compliant library; for challenge response, verify signature over exact concatenation of (nonce_bytes || timestamp_as_8-byte-big-endian || verifier_domain_utf8_bytes); for delegation token, verify COSE Sign1 signature using Master Key from DNS

DNS TXT record parsing: query _locd.<domain> TXT record, parse semicolon-delimited fields (v, k, p, t, exp, rev), base64url-decode the p field (no padding) to obtain 32-byte Ed25519 public key, validate v=locd1 and k=ed25519

DNSSEC validation: verify complete chain of trust from root to the queried record; use a DNSSEC-validating resolver or perform validation in-application; reject with dnssec_invalid if any signature in the chain fails

DNS-over-HTTPS: clients SHOULD use DoH (RFC 8484) for identity lookups to prevent network observers from seeing which identities are being verified; configure with system or user-specified DoH resolver

Revocation DNS record parsing: query _locd-revoke.<domain> TXT record, parse format v=locd-revoke1; ids=<comma-separated-UUIDs>; t=<timestamp>, check if delegation_id from presented token appears in the ids list

Supplementary revocation list fetching: HTTP GET to rev URL from identity record, parse signed JSON with fields v, identity, revocations array (each with delegation_id, revoked_at, reason), published_at, signature; verify Ed25519 signature by Master Key before trusting the list

Nonce generation: use cryptographically secure random number generator (e.g., /dev/urandom, CSPRNG) to produce exactly 32 bytes per challenge; store issued nonces with timestamps for replay detection; expire stored nonces after 60 seconds
Timestamp tolerance: accept timestamps within 60 seconds of local time in either direction; requires reasonably synchronized clocks (NTP recommended); reject with timestamp_skew if outside tolerance

Domain pattern matching for service scope: support exact domain match (api.example.com) and wildcard prefix match (*.example.com matches api.example.com but not example.com); empty/absent services array permits all domains

Sub-delegation chain validation: iterate chain root-first; for each link verify: (a) delegator field matches delegate of previous link (or Master Key for first), (b) COSE Sign1 signature valid by delegator key, (c) services subset of parent services, (d) actions subset of parent actions, (e) expires_at <= parent expires_at, (f) total chain depth <= 3

Max uses counter: maintain persistent or semi-persistent storage keyed by delegation_id tracking usage count; increment atomically on successful verification; compare against max_uses field (0 or absent means unlimited); handle concurrent verification attempts safely

Public key cache implementation: cache keyed by identity domain; store public key, resolution timestamp, DNS TTL, and optional delegation expires_at; eviction policy: min(DNS TTL expiry, delegation expires_at); on DNS failure, serve cached entry if within TTL (degraded mode); log cache hits/misses for observability

Wire framing: each message transmitted as 4-byte big-endian unsigned 32-bit integer length prefix followed by CBOR payload bytes; maximum message size SHOULD be enforced (recommended 64KB) to prevent memory exhaustion; handle partial reads and frame reassembly

Mutual authentication flow: after Claimant is verified, if mutual auth is requested, Verifier sends its own HELLO with its identity domain, Claimant issues CHALLENGE, Verifier signs RESPONSE with its own Device Key and delegation token, Claimant verifies; RECOMMENDED for mesh connections, OPTIONAL for service connections

TPM attestation validation (optional): if delegation token field 10 (attestation) is present, validate TPM 2.0 attestation quote against known TPM certificate authorities; return attestation_failed if quote does not validate; this is OPTIONAL and MAY be skipped if field is absent
Identity record expiry: if the identity TXT record includes an exp field and the current time exceeds that timestamp, return identity_expired; if exp is absent, the identity does not expire

Replay protection: Verifier MUST track recently issued nonces and reject any RESPONSE containing a nonce not in the active set; nonces MUST be single-use; clear nonce from active set after successful verification or after 60-second timeout

Concurrent session handling: Verifier may process multiple verification flows simultaneously; nonce storage and max_uses counters must be thread-safe; each verification flow is independent and identified by its unique nonce

Error ordering: verification checks SHOULD be performed in this order to return the most specific reason code: (1) DNS resolution, (2) DNSSEC validation, (3) identity record parsing, (4) identity expiry, (5) delegation signature, (6) delegation expiry, (7) delegation revocation, (8) service scope, (9) action scope, (10) max uses, (11) sub-delegation chain, (12) response signature/nonce, (13) timestamp tolerance, (14) attestation

Key rotation awareness: if a _locd-rotate.<domain> record exists with a valid rotation announcement, the Verifier SHOULD accept delegations signed by either the old or new Master Key during the 7-day transition period
Verification Layer.api_contract: Core interfaces: (1) VerificationService with methods: handleHello(hello: CborHelloMessage) -> CborChallengeMessage; handleResponse(response: CborResponseMessage, pendingChallenge: ChallengeState) -> CborResultMessage; initiateMutualAuth(verifierIdentity: string, verifierDeviceKey: bytes, verifierDelegation: CoseSign1Token) -> CborHelloMessage. (2) IdentityResolver with methods: resolveIdentity(domain: string) -> {publicKey: bytes, timestamp: number, expiry?: number, revocationUrl?: string}; checkRevocation(domain: string, delegationId: string) -> boolean; getCachedIdentity(domain: string) -> CachedIdentity | null. (3) DelegationVerifier with methods: verifyDelegation(token: CoseSign1Token, expectedMasterKey: bytes, serviceDomain: string, action?: string) -> {valid: boolean, reason: string}; verifyChain(chain: CoseSign1Token[], masterKey: bytes, serviceDomain: string, action?: string) -> {valid: boolean, reason: string}; trackUsage(delegationId: string, maxUses: number) -> boolean. (4) ChallengeManager with methods: createChallenge(verifierDomain: string) -> {nonce: bytes, timestamp: number, cborMessage: bytes}; validateNonce(nonce: bytes) -> boolean; consumeNonce(nonce: bytes) -> void. (5) PublicKeyCache with methods: get(domain: string) -> CachedKey | null; set(domain: string, key: bytes, ttl: number, delegationExpiry?: number) -> void; evictExpired() -> void. (6) WireFramer with methods: encode(cborPayload: bytes) -> bytes (prepends 4-byte length); decode(stream: ReadableStream) -> AsyncIterator<bytes> (yields complete CBOR payloads). All public-facing methods return structured results with reason codes rather than throwing exceptions for verification failures.
dependencies: CBOR library (RFC 8949 compliant) for encoding/decoding all protocol messages
COSE library (RFC 9052 compliant) for Sign1 structure creation, parsing, and signature verification
Ed25519 cryptographic library (RFC 8032 compliant) for signature generation and verification
X25519 library (RFC 7748) for session key agreement in mutual authentication scenarios
DNS resolver library with DNSSEC validation support (RFC 4033-4035)
DNS-over-HTTPS client library (RFC 8484) for privacy-preserving identity lookups
HTTP client for fetching supplementary revocation lists from rev URLs
Cryptographically secure random number generator for 32-byte nonce production
Base64url encoder/decoder (no padding variant) for public key serialization
UUID v4 library for delegation ID handling and comparison
Identity Layer module (Section 5) for DNS record format parsing and identity publication
Delegation Layer module (Section 6) for delegation token format definitions and CBOR map schema
Revocation Layer module (Section 8) for revocation record format and revocation list schema
Mesh Connectivity Layer module (Section 9) for WireGuard public key exchange after successful verification
TPM 2.0 attestation verification library (optional, for attestation field validation)
Argon2id library (optional, only if recovery-adjacent verification is needed)
Thread-safe key-value store or concurrent map for nonce tracking, usage counters, and public key caching

Revocation Layer.summary: The Revocation Layer implements a three-tier revocation system for Loc'd delegation tokens, providing defense-in-depth against compromised devices and keys. Layer 1 relies on short-lived delegations (24-hour default expiry) for passive revocation without any active intervention. Layer 2 publishes explicit revocation records via DNS TXT records at _locd-revoke.<domain> for authoritative, globally-queryable revocation. Layer 3 provides a supplementary HTTPS revocation list (signed JSON document) at the rev URL specified in the identity record for fast propagation when DNS updates lag. The module also implements Master Key rotation for catastrophic compromise scenarios, publishing rotation records at _locd-rotate.<domain> signed by the old key, invalidating all existing delegations, and supporting a 7-day transition period. All revocation statements are cryptographically signed by the Master Key to prevent unauthorized revocation attacks.
features: Layer 1: Passive revocation via short-lived delegation tokens with configurable expiry (default 24 hours, max 30 days)
Layer 2: DNS-based revocation records at _locd-revoke.<domain> containing comma-separated delegation UUIDs and publication timestamp
Layer 3: HTTPS supplementary revocation list as signed JSON document with per-delegation revocation metadata (delegation_id, revoked_at, reason)
Revocation statement creation signed by Master Key in secure enclave with biometric/PIN gating
Dual-channel revocation checking during verification (DNS TXT record AND HTTPS endpoint when rev URL is present)
Five structured revocation reasons: device_lost, device_compromised, key_rotation, scope_change, user_initiated
Master Key rotation flow: generate new key, publish rotation record signed by old key, invalidate all existing delegations
DNS rotation record at _locd-rotate.<domain> with old pubkey, new pubkey, timestamp, and signature by old key
7-day recommended transition period for Master Key rotation where both old and new keys are recognized
Revocation list Ed25519 signature verification to prevent tampering with HTTPS revocation endpoint
Revocation propagation to mesh-connected devices via WireGuard sync channel
Graceful degradation when DNS or HTTPS revocation endpoints are unreachable
acceptance_criteria: When a delegation token's expires_at timestamp has passed, any verification attempt using that token MUST return delegation_expired without requiring active revocation
When a delegation UUID is added to the DNS TXT record at _locd-revoke.<domain>, verifiers querying that record MUST reject the delegation with reason delegation_revoked
The DNS revocation record MUST conform to format: v=locd-revoke1; ids=<comma-separated-UUIDs>; t=<unix-timestamp>
When a revocation list is published at the HTTPS rev URL, it MUST be a JSON document containing v, identity, revocations array, published_at, and signature fields
The HTTPS revocation list signature MUST be verifiable using the Master Key published in the identity DNS record
Verifiers MUST check both DNS revocation records AND the HTTPS revocation endpoint (if rev URL exists in identity record) during every verification flow
If a delegation UUID appears in either the DNS revocation record OR the HTTPS revocation list, the delegation MUST be rejected
Each revocation entry in the HTTPS list MUST include a valid reason from the enumerated set: device_lost, device_compromised, key_rotation, scope_change, user_initiated
Revocation statements MUST be signed by the Master Key; any revocation statement not signed by the corresponding Master Key MUST be ignored
Master Key rotation record at _locd-rotate.<domain> MUST contain old pubkey, new pubkey, timestamp, and valid Ed25519 signature by the old key
After Master Key rotation, all delegation tokens signed by the old Master Key MUST be rejected by verifiers that have observed the rotation record
During the 7-day transition period after Master Key rotation, verifiers MUST accept delegations signed by the new Master Key
If DNS is unreachable during revocation checking, verifiers MUST use cached revocation data (if within TTL) and SHOULD log a degraded-mode warning
Revocation records published to DNS MUST use a TTL of 60 seconds to ensure fast propagation
When a user initiates revocation on their phone, the revocation MUST be published to both DNS and HTTPS endpoints within one protocol round-trip
Mesh-connected devices MUST receive revocation list updates via WireGuard sync within 60 seconds of publication
Adding a new revocation MUST NOT remove previously revoked delegation IDs from the DNS record or HTTPS list
The HTTPS revocation list MUST include a published_at timestamp that is more recent than or equal to the most recent revoked_at in the list
Verifiers MUST NOT cache revocation data beyond the DNS TTL (60 seconds for revocation records) or the delegation token's expires_at, whichever is sooner
A delegation token that has been revoked MUST remain rejected even if the DNS revocation record is temporarily unavailable, provided the verifier has previously observed the revocation
technical_spec: DNS revocation TXT record format: v=locd-revoke1; ids=<UUID1>,<UUID2>,...; t=<unix-timestamp> published at _locd-revoke.<domain>
DNS revocation record TTL MUST be 60 seconds per specification Section 5.5

HTTPS revocation list is a JSON document with fields: v (string, 'locd-revoke-list-v1'), identity (string, domain), revocations (array of objects), published_at (uint, unix timestamp), signature (string, base64url Ed25519 signature)
Each revocation entry contains: delegation_id (UUID v4 string), revoked_at (uint, unix timestamp), reason (enum string)
Revocation list signature covers the canonical JSON serialization of the document excluding the signature field itself
Ed25519 signature for revocation list uses the same Master Key published in the _locd.<domain> identity record
DNS rotation record format: v=locd-rotate1; old=<base64url-old-pubkey>; new=<base64url-new-pubkey>; t=<unix-timestamp>; sig=<base64url-Ed25519-signature-by-old-key>
Rotation signature is computed over the concatenation: old-pubkey || new-pubkey || timestamp (big-endian uint64)
Verifiers MUST check _locd-revoke.<domain> via DNSSEC-validated query; reject if DNSSEC validation fails
Verifiers SHOULD check HTTPS revocation list via TLS 1.3; verify Ed25519 signature before trusting content

DNS record size limit consideration: TXT records have a practical limit of ~64KB; for identities with many revoked delegations, the HTTPS list is the primary store and DNS holds the most recent revocations
Implement local revocation cache with TTL-based expiry: cache DNS results for 60 seconds, cache HTTPS results for 60 seconds or until next verification cycle
Revocation check order: (1) check local cache, (2) query DNS _locd-revoke record, (3) fetch HTTPS rev URL if present in identity record, (4) reject if delegation_id found in any source
Master Key rotation invalidates ALL existing delegations by changing the delegator public key; verifiers comparing delegation.delegator against DNS identity will find a mismatch
During transition period: verifiers that see _locd-rotate record MUST accept delegations signed by EITHER old or new key for up to 7 days after rotation timestamp
After transition period (t + 7 days): verifiers MUST only accept delegations signed by the new Master Key
Mesh sync of revocation data: serialize current revocation list, encrypt via WireGuard tunnel, broadcast to all mesh peers
Graceful degradation: if both DNS and HTTPS are unreachable, use last-known revocation data if within 2x TTL; beyond that, verifier policy determines accept/reject (SHOULD reject)
Concurrent revocation publishing: DNS update and HTTPS update should be initiated in parallel to minimize propagation delay
Revocation reason is informational for audit/UX purposes; the verification outcome is binary (revoked or not) regardless of reason
Revocation Layer.api_contract: Core interfaces: (1) RevocationManager - creates and signs revocation statements, publishes to DNS and HTTPS endpoints, manages local revocation cache. Methods: revokeDelegate(delegationId, reason) -> RevocationStatement; publishToDNS(domain, revocationIds) -> bool; publishToHTTPS(revUrl, revocationList) -> bool; checkRevocation(delegationId, domain, revUrl?) -> RevocationStatus. (2) RotationManager - handles Master Key rotation lifecycle. Methods: initiateRotation(oldKey, newKey) -> RotationRecord; publishRotation(domain, rotationRecord) -> bool; isInTransitionPeriod(domain) -> bool; getActiveKey(domain) -> PublicKey. (3) RevocationCache - local TTL-based cache for revocation data. Methods: get(domain) -> CachedRevocations?; set(domain, revocations, ttl) -> void; invalidate(domain) -> void. (4) Verification integration points: verifyDelegation() in the Verification Layer MUST call checkRevocation() as part of its validation pipeline, after signature verification and before returning VERIFIED. DNS record operations use standard DNS UPDATE protocol or provider-specific APIs. HTTPS revocation list served as static JSON at .well-known/locd/revocations path.
dependencies: Identity Layer - provides Master Key public key via DNS for signature verification of revocation statements and lists
Delegation Layer - provides delegation token format and delegation_id (UUID v4) used as revocation identifiers
Verification Layer - integrates revocation checking into the challenge-response verification pipeline (step 6 of verification flow)
DNS infrastructure with DNSSEC support for publishing and querying _locd-revoke and _locd-rotate TXT records
HTTPS hosting for .well-known/locd/revocations endpoint (static file or dynamic endpoint)
Ed25519 cryptographic library (RFC 8032) for signing and verifying revocation statements and rotation records
Mesh Connectivity Layer - for propagating revocation updates to mesh-connected devices via WireGuard sync
Secure enclave / TPM access for Master Key signing operations (revocation statements must be signed in hardware)
DNS-over-HTTPS (RFC 8484) client for privacy-preserving revocation record lookups
Base64url encoding/decoding for public key serialization in DNS records

Wire Protocol & Transport.summary: The Wire Protocol & Transport module implements the transport-agnostic framing and connection management layer for the Loc'd protocol. It provides length-prefixed CBOR message framing (4-byte big-endian uint32 length header followed by variable-length CBOR payload), supports multiple transport backends (raw TCP, WebSocket, HTTP/2, HTTP/3), and handles protocol version negotiation through the HELLO/version_unsupported exchange. This module serves as the foundation that upper protocol layers (Verification, Delegation, Mesh) build upon, ensuring they remain completely transport-independent. It manages the full connection lifecycle including establishment, keepalives, graceful shutdown, and error handling across all supported transports.
features: Length-prefixed binary framing with 4-byte big-endian uint32 length header followed by CBOR-encoded payload bytes
Raw TCP transport backend with configurable bind address and port (default port TBD per IANA registration)
WebSocket transport backend for browser-based clients with binary frame mode
HTTP/2 and HTTP/3 transport backends for web service integration using bidirectional streaming
Transport abstraction interface that exposes uniform send/receive semantics regardless of underlying transport
Protocol version negotiation: HELLO message includes protocol version field, responder returns version_unsupported with list of supported versions on mismatch
Connection lifecycle management: establishment handshake, authenticated session binding, and graceful shutdown with drain semantics
Keepalive mechanism with configurable interval and timeout to detect dead connections across all transports
Frame validation: reject frames exceeding maximum payload size, malformed CBOR, or zero-length payloads
Backpressure and flow control: per-connection write buffering with configurable high-water marks
Connection multiplexing support for HTTP/2 and HTTP/3 transports using stream identifiers
TLS requirement enforcement: all transports except localhost loopback MUST use TLS 1.3 or transport-native encryption
acceptance_criteria: A sender can construct a frame with a 4-byte big-endian uint32 length prefix followed by a CBOR payload, and a receiver on any supported transport correctly parses the length prefix and extracts the exact CBOR payload bytes
When a HELLO message specifies protocol version 'locd1' and the receiver supports 'locd1', the connection proceeds without a version_unsupported response

When a HELLO message specifies an unsupported protocol version (e.g., 'locd99'), the receiver responds with a version_unsupported message containing an array of its supported version strings and then closes the connection
A raw TCP transport accepts connections on a configurable port and correctly frames/deframes length-prefixed CBOR messages over the TCP byte stream
A WebSocket transport sends and receives length-prefixed CBOR messages using binary WebSocket frames, and rejects text frames with an error
An HTTP/2 transport sends and receives length-prefixed CBOR messages over a bidirectional HTTP/2 stream with content-type application/cbor
An HTTP/3 transport sends and receives length-prefixed CBOR messages over a bidirectional HTTP/3 stream with content-type application/cbor
Upper-layer code (Verification, Mesh) can send and receive CBOR messages through a transport-agnostic interface without knowing which transport is in use
A frame with a length prefix indicating a payload larger than the configured maximum frame size (default 1 MiB) is rejected with an oversized_frame error and the connection is closed
A frame with a length prefix of zero bytes is rejected with an empty_frame error
A frame whose CBOR payload fails to decode is rejected with a malformed_payload error and the connection is closed

When no data is exchanged for the configured keepalive interval (default 30 seconds), the transport sends a keepalive probe; if no response is received within the keepalive timeout (default 10 seconds), the connection is closed

Graceful shutdown sends a close notification, stops accepting new messages, flushes pending outbound messages, and then closes the underlying transport within a configurable drain timeout (default 5 seconds)
When the remote peer closes the connection unexpectedly, the local side detects the closure within the keepalive timeout and emits a connection_lost event to upper layers
All non-loopback connections enforce TLS 1.3 minimum; attempting a plaintext connection to a non-loopback address results in a tls_required error
Under backpressure (write buffer exceeds high-water mark), the transport signals the upper layer to pause sending and resumes when the buffer drains below the low-water mark
Multiple concurrent connections on the same transport do not interfere with each other; each connection maintains independent framing state, keepalive timers, and buffers
The HELLO message round-trip completes within 5 seconds on any transport; if not, the connection times out with a handshake_timeout error
technical_spec: Frame format: [uint32 big-endian length (4 bytes)] [CBOR payload (length bytes)]. Maximum payload size configurable, default 1 MiB (1,048,576 bytes). Length field represents payload size only, not including the 4-byte header itself.
CBOR encoding/decoding MUST comply with RFC 8949. All protocol messages (HELLO, CHALLENGE, RESPONSE, RESULT, keepalive, close) are CBOR maps with integer keys as defined in the Loc'd specification.
Raw TCP transport: single connection per TCP socket. Framing handles TCP stream reassembly (partial reads). Uses Nagle-disabled sockets (TCP_NODELAY) for low-latency message delivery.

WebSocket transport: each WebSocket connection maps to one Loc'd connection. MUST use binary frame opcode (0x02). Each WebSocket message contains exactly one length-prefixed CBOR frame. Subprotocol identifier: 'locd.v1'.

HTTP/2 transport: uses a single bidirectional stream per connection. Content-Type: application/cbor. Each HTTP/2 DATA frame may contain partial or multiple length-prefixed messages; the framing layer handles reassembly.
HTTP/3 transport: same semantics as HTTP/2 but over QUIC. Benefits from QUIC's 0-RTT connection establishment and built-in multiplexing. Each QUIC stream maps to one Loc'd connection.
Protocol negotiation message (version_unsupported) is a CBOR map: {1: 'locd-version-unsupported', 2: ['locd1']} where key 2 is an array of supported version strings.
Keepalive messages are CBOR maps: {1: 'locd-ping'} and {1: 'locd-pong'}. These are transport-layer messages that do not propagate to upper layers.
Close notification is a CBOR map: {1: 'locd-close', 2: 'reason_string'}. Reason strings include: 'shutdown', 'timeout', 'error', 'version_unsupported'.

Transport abstraction interface exposes: connect(address, transport_type) -> Connection, listen(address, transport_type) -> Listener, Connection.send(cbor_bytes), Connection.receive() -> cbor_bytes, Connection.close(reason), Listener.accept() -> Connection.
Connection state machine: CONNECTING -> NEGOTIATING (HELLO exchange) -> ESTABLISHED -> DRAINING -> CLOSED. Invalid state transitions raise protocol errors.
TLS configuration: TLS 1.3 minimum, certificate verification required for non-mesh connections. For mesh connections, WireGuard provides encryption so TLS is not required on the inner tunnel.

Error codes returned as CBOR maps: {1: 'locd-error', 2: 'error_code', 3: 'human_readable_message'}. Error codes: oversized_frame, empty_frame, malformed_payload, tls_required, handshake_timeout, keepalive_timeout, version_unsupported, internal_error.

Backpressure: configurable write buffer high-water mark (default 64 KiB) and low-water mark (default 16 KiB). When high-water mark is reached, send() returns a backpressure signal. Upper layer MUST respect this signal.
All transports MUST support both IPv4 and IPv6 addressing. DNS resolution for connection targets uses system resolver with preference for IPv6 (happy eyeballs / RFC 8305).
Connection metadata tracked per connection: transport type, remote address, connection start time, bytes sent/received, frames sent/received, last activity timestamp, current state.
Wire Protocol & Transport.api_contract: Core interfaces: TransportListener with methods bind(address: string, port: int) and accept() returning a Connection; TransportConnector with method connect(address: string, port: int, transport: enum[tcp, websocket, h2, h3]) returning a Connection; Connection with methods send(payload: bytes), receive() returning bytes, close(reason: string), and properties state (enum[connecting, negotiating, established, draining, closed]), remote_address, transport_type, and stats (ConnectionStats); ConnectionStats with fields bytes_sent, bytes_received, frames_sent, frames_received, connected_at, last_activity. Events emitted: on_connection(Connection), on_message(Connection, bytes), on_close(Connection, reason), on_error(Connection, error_code, message), on_backpressure(Connection, bool). Factory function create_transport(config) returns appropriate TransportListener or TransportConnector based on configuration. Configuration includes: transport type, bind address, port, max_frame_size, keepalive_interval, keepalive_timeout, drain_timeout, tls_cert_path, tls_key_path, write_buffer_high_water, write_buffer_low_water.
dependencies: CBOR encoding/decoding library compliant with RFC 8949
WebSocket library supporting binary frames and subprotocol negotiation
HTTP/3 / QUIC library supporting bidirectional streams (optional, for h3 transport)
Operating system TCP/UDP socket APIs with IPv4 and IPv6 support
Async I/O runtime for concurrent connection handling (e.g., asyncio, tokio, libuv depending on implementation language)
No direct dependency on upper Loc'd layers (Identity, Delegation, Verification, Mesh) - those layers depend on this module

Mesh Connectivity.summary: The Mesh Connectivity module provides encrypted peer-to-peer networking for Loc'd-authenticated devices using WireGuard tunnels. After successful identity verification via the Verification Layer, devices exchange WireGuard public keys, configure peer entries, and establish encrypted tunnels without exposing publicly accessible ports. The module includes a self-hosted coordination service compatible with the Headscale API for IP address and port mapping storage, peer-to-peer discovery for small meshes (2-5 devices), UDP hole-punching with STUN/TURN relay fallback, and device-to-device synchronization of delegation tokens, revocation lists, and encrypted Legacy Bridge credentials over the mesh.
features: Post-verification WireGuard key exchange: After RESULT message containing Verifier's WireGuard public key, Claimant responds with its WireGuard public key, and both sides configure WireGuard peer entries to establish the tunnel
Self-hosted coordination service compatible with Headscale API that stores current IP addresses and port mappings, authenticates via Loc'd identity, and passes no traffic through the coordinator
Peer-to-peer discovery mode for small meshes (2-5 devices) where address info is exchanged directly via WireGuard mesh after one initial bootstrapping connection
UDP hole-punching for NAT traversal enabling direct peer connections without open ports
STUN/TURN relay fallback when direct UDP hole-punching fails, ensuring relay sees only encrypted WireGuard traffic
Enforcement that mesh devices never listen on publicly accessible ports; all connections use hole-punching, relay, or coordination-based rendezvous
Device-to-device synchronization over WireGuard mesh for current delegation token sets, revocation lists, and Legacy Bridge credentials encrypted to each device's TPM key
Mutual authentication support for mesh connections using the bidirectional verification protocol from Section 7.3
Mesh topology management including peer addition, removal, and reconnection handling
Graceful degradation when coordination service is unreachable, falling back to cached peer information
acceptance_criteria: Given a successful identity verification (RESULT with verified=true), when the Verifier sends its WireGuard public key in the RESULT message, then the Claimant responds with its own WireGuard public key and both sides configure WireGuard peer entries within 5 seconds

Given two devices with valid Loc'd identities and delegation tokens, when they complete the key exchange, then a WireGuard tunnel is established and encrypted traffic can flow bidirectionally between them

Given a self-hosted coordination service is running, when a device authenticates via Loc'd identity, then the service stores the device's current IP address and port mapping and returns peer information for other authorized devices in the mesh
Given the coordination service is running, when traffic flows between mesh devices, then zero bytes of application traffic pass through the coordinator (coordinator handles only metadata/rendezvous)
Given two devices behind NAT, when they attempt to connect via the mesh, then UDP hole-punching is attempted first before falling back to STUN/TURN relay
Given UDP hole-punching fails between two NATted devices, when the STUN/TURN relay is used as fallback, then the relay handles only encrypted WireGuard packets and cannot decrypt any payload
Given a mesh device, when its network interfaces are inspected, then no publicly accessible listening ports are exposed for Loc'd mesh connectivity

Given a mesh of 2-5 devices, when peer-to-peer discovery mode is enabled, then devices can exchange address information directly via existing WireGuard connections after one initial bootstrap connection
Given two devices in a mesh, when device-to-device sync is triggered, then the current delegation token set is synchronized over the WireGuard tunnel
Given two devices in a mesh, when revocation lists are updated on one device, then the updated revocation list is synchronized to the other device over the WireGuard tunnel within 60 seconds

Given Legacy Bridge credentials exist on one device, when cross-device sync occurs, then credentials are encrypted using a DH-derived shared secret between Device Keys, transmitted over the WireGuard mesh, and re-encrypted to the receiver's TPM-bound key

Given a mesh connection between two devices, when mutual authentication is requested, then both sides complete the Loc'd verification protocol in both directions before the tunnel is marked as fully authenticated
Given the coordination service becomes unreachable, when a device attempts to connect to a known peer, then it falls back to cached peer information and attempts direct connection
Given a device's delegation token is revoked, when the revocation propagates via mesh sync, then other mesh peers remove the revoked device's WireGuard peer entry and refuse new connections from it
Given a WireGuard tunnel is established, when the session exceeds 24 hours, then re-authentication is required per the Session Key lifetime maximum specified in Section 4.3

Given the coordination service API, when a client makes requests using the Headscale-compatible API format, then the service responds with correctly formatted responses matching Headscale API conventions
technical_spec: WireGuard key exchange occurs as a follow-up to the CBOR-encoded RESULT message from Section 7.2; the Claimant sends a CBOR message containing its WireGuard X25519 public key after receiving the Verifier's key in the RESULT payload (field 4: bytes)
WireGuard peer configuration must set AllowedIPs to the specific peer's mesh-internal IP address, not broad CIDR ranges, to enforce least-privilege routing
Coordination service implements a subset of the Headscale API covering node registration, key distribution, peer list retrieval, and DERP map configuration endpoints
Coordination service authenticates all API requests using Loc'd identity verification (challenge-response from Section 7.1) rather than API keys or OAuth tokens
Coordination service stores only ephemeral network metadata (IP addresses, ports, WireGuard public keys, last-seen timestamps) and must not store or log traffic content
UDP hole-punching implementation follows STUN (RFC 5389) for NAT type detection and binding discovery, with ICE-like candidate gathering to find the best connection path
TURN relay (RFC 5766) is the final fallback; relay allocations are authenticated via Loc'd identity and relay only encrypted WireGuard UDP packets, making the relay a zero-knowledge transport
Peer-to-peer discovery for small meshes uses gossip-style address exchange over existing WireGuard tunnels, where each peer broadcasts its known peer list to directly connected peers
Device-to-device sync uses a CRDT-based or vector-clock-based merge strategy for delegation token sets and revocation lists to handle concurrent updates without conflicts

Legacy Bridge credential sync implements the cross-device flow from Section 10.4: DH key agreement (X25519) between Device Keys produces a shared secret, credentials encrypted with XChaCha20-Poly1305 using HKDF-SHA256 derived key, transmitted over WireGuard mesh, re-encrypted to receiver's TPM-bound key, shared secret discarded after sync
All mesh-internal communication uses CBOR encoding consistent with the wire format specified in Section 13.1: 4-byte big-endian length prefix followed by CBOR payload

Mesh topology state is maintained locally on each device with peer entries including: WireGuard public key, last known endpoint (IP:port), delegation token reference, last-seen timestamp, and connection quality metrics
NAT traversal must support symmetric NAT scenarios where both peers are behind restrictive NATs, using the TURN relay as the guaranteed fallback path
Coordination service must be fully self-hostable with no dependency on external infrastructure; provide Docker container and binary distribution options
WireGuard interface management must handle dynamic peer addition and removal without restarting the WireGuard interface or disrupting existing tunnels

Mesh reconnection logic must implement exponential backoff with jitter (initial 1s, max 60s) when a peer becomes unreachable, with immediate reconnection attempt when network state changes are detected
Session Key (Tier 3) lifetime enforcement: track tunnel establishment time and force re-authentication after 24 hours maximum as specified in Section 4.3
Mesh Connectivity.api_contract: Coordination Service API (Headscale-compatible subset): POST /api/v1/node/register - Register device with Loc'd identity and WireGuard public key, authenticated via challenge-response; GET /api/v1/node/list - List all authorized peers in the mesh with their current endpoints; PUT /api/v1/node/{nodeId}/endpoint - Update a device's current IP address and port mapping; DELETE /api/v1/node/{nodeId} - Remove a device from the mesh; GET /api/v1/derp-map - Retrieve DERP/relay server configuration; POST /api/v1/node/{nodeId}/sync - Trigger delegation token and revocation list sync to specified peer. Internal Mesh Sync Protocol (over WireGuard): SYNC_REQUEST {type, vector_clock, payload_hash} - Request sync of delegation tokens, revocation lists, or credentials; SYNC_RESPONSE {type, entries, vector_clock} - Response with updated entries; SYNC_ACK {type, vector_clock} - Acknowledgment of received sync data. WireGuard Key Exchange Extension: POST_RESULT_KEY_EXCHANGE {type: 'locd-wg-key-v1', wireguard_pubkey: bytes} - CBOR message sent by Claimant after receiving RESULT with Verifier's WireGuard key.
dependencies: Verification Layer (Section 7) - Must complete identity verification before tunnel establishment; consumes RESULT message output
Identity Layer (Section 5) - DNS-published Master Key used for coordination service authentication
Delegation Layer (Section 6) - Delegation tokens synchronized across mesh; scope constraints determine mesh access permissions
Revocation Layer (Section 8) - Revocation lists synchronized across mesh; revoked delegations trigger peer removal
Legacy Bridge Layer (Section 10) - Encrypted credentials synchronized across mesh using cross-device sync protocol
WireGuard kernel module or userspace implementation (wireguard-go) for tunnel management
STUN server implementation or library (RFC 5389) for NAT traversal and binding discovery
TURN server implementation or library (RFC 5766) for relay fallback
CBOR encoding/decoding library (RFC 8949) for wire format serialization
COSE signing library (RFC 9052) for verifying delegation tokens during coordination service authentication
X25519 key agreement (RFC 7748) for DH-based credential sync shared secret derivation
XChaCha20-Poly1305 and HKDF-SHA256 for credential encryption during cross-device sync
TPM 2.0 or Secure Enclave interface for hardware-bound Device Key operations during mesh authentication

Legacy Bridge.summary: The Legacy Bridge module provides secure credential management for services that do not natively support the Loc'd protocol. It encrypts passwords, API keys, and OAuth tokens using XChaCha20-Poly1305 with keys derived via HKDF-SHA256 from the device's TPM-bound Device Key, ensuring credentials are never stored in plaintext, never transmitted to external servers, and never decryptable without physical access to the TPM. The module implements just-in-time credential injection where plaintext exists in memory only during the moment of use, cross-device credential synchronization over the WireGuard mesh using ephemeral Diffie-Hellman shared secrets, and a dashboard indicator system (green/yellow/red) that communicates authentication security posture to the user at a glance.
features: TPM-bound credential encryption using XChaCha20-Poly1305 with HKDF-SHA256 key derivation from Device Key
Credential vault supporting multiple credential types: passwords, API keys, OAuth tokens (access + refresh), and arbitrary secret strings
Just-in-time credential injection that decrypts within TPM trust boundary and injects into outbound connections with minimal plaintext lifetime
Cross-device credential synchronization via X25519 DH key exchange between Device Keys, encrypted transit over WireGuard mesh, and receiver-side re-encryption to local TPM key
Per-service credential metadata tracking: service domain, credential type, creation date, last used, last rotated, sync status, and security posture

Dashboard security indicators: green (native Loc'd auth), yellow (Legacy Bridge managed credentials), red (known security issue such as expired OAuth token, weak password detected, or credential compromise alert)
Credential lifecycle management: add, update, rotate, delete, and bulk export (encrypted) for backup purposes
Automatic OAuth token refresh using stored refresh tokens without exposing credentials to the user or any external service
Credential health checks: detect expired tokens, weak passwords, reused credentials across services, and credentials approaching rotation policy deadlines
Memory safety: cryptographic zeroization of plaintext credential buffers immediately after injection, enforced maximum in-memory lifetime
acceptance_criteria: Given a new credential is stored, when the credential vault file is read from disk, then the credential payload MUST be encrypted with XChaCha20-Poly1305 and the encryption key MUST be derived via HKDF-SHA256 from the TPM-bound Device Key with a unique per-credential salt

Given a credential is stored, when the TPM hardware is not present or accessible, then all decryption attempts MUST fail and return an explicit TPM-unavailable error rather than falling back to software decryption

Given a credential injection is requested, when the credential is decrypted for use, then the plaintext MUST exist in memory for no longer than the duration of the injection operation and MUST be zeroized immediately after the outbound request is constructed
Given a credential injection completes, when inspecting process memory after injection, then no plaintext credential material SHALL remain in any application-accessible buffer

Given two devices A and B are in the same WireGuard mesh, when device A initiates credential sync to device B, then an X25519 DH exchange between their Device Keys produces an ephemeral shared secret, credentials are encrypted with that shared secret for transit, and the shared secret is discarded after the sync completes on both sides

Given device B receives synced credentials, when the sync payload is decrypted, then each credential MUST be immediately re-encrypted using device B's own TPM-bound Device Key before being written to persistent storage
Given a credential sync is attempted, when the WireGuard mesh is unavailable, then the sync operation MUST fail cleanly with an appropriate error and MUST NOT fall back to any non-mesh transport
Given a service uses native Loc'd authentication, when the dashboard renders that service entry, then the indicator MUST be green
Given a service uses Legacy Bridge credentials, when the dashboard renders that service entry and the credential is valid and not flagged, then the indicator MUST be yellow

Given a service has an expired OAuth token, a credential flagged as potentially compromised, or a detected weak password, when the dashboard renders that service entry, then the indicator MUST be red with a human-readable reason string

Given a credential of type OAuth with a refresh token, when the access token expires, then the system MUST automatically attempt token refresh and update the stored credential without user interaction
Given a credential is deleted, when the vault file is rewritten, then the deleted credential's ciphertext MUST be overwritten and not recoverable from the vault file
Given multiple credentials exist for different services, when a health check runs, then it MUST detect and flag any credentials reused across two or more services

Given credential sync from device A to device B, when the DH shared secret is derived, then the shared secret MUST NOT be written to persistent storage on either device at any point during the sync process

Given an attacker obtains a copy of the credential vault file without TPM access, when they attempt to decrypt any credential, then the attempt MUST be computationally infeasible without the TPM-bound key material
technical_spec: Encryption algorithm: XChaCha20-Poly1305 (24-byte nonce, 32-byte key, 16-byte authentication tag) for all credential-at-rest encryption

Key derivation: HKDF-SHA256 with TPM-bound Device Key as input keying material, per-credential 32-byte random salt, and context string 'locd-legacy-bridge-v1-credential' to produce a 32-byte encryption key
Each credential record stores: 24-byte nonce, 32-byte salt, 16-byte auth tag, variable-length ciphertext, and unencrypted metadata (service domain, credential type enum, timestamps)
Credential type enum: PASSWORD, API_KEY, OAUTH_ACCESS, OAUTH_REFRESH, OAUTH_PAIR, BEARER_TOKEN, CUSTOM_SECRET

Vault file format: CBOR-encoded map keyed by service domain, with each entry containing an array of credential records, stored at a configurable local filesystem path (default: ~/.locd/bridge/vault.cbor)

TPM interaction for decryption: submit HKDF input keying material derivation request to TPM, receive derived key in sealed memory, perform XChaCha20-Poly1305 decryption in user space, zeroize key buffer immediately after decryption

Credential injection architecture: credential is decrypted into a pinned, non-swappable memory page (mlock'd), injected into the outbound request (HTTP header, request body, or connection parameter depending on credential type), and the page is cryptographically zeroized (not just freed) after the request is constructed

Cross-device sync protocol: initiator sends sync-request message over WireGuard mesh containing initiator's ephemeral X25519 public key; responder replies with its ephemeral X25519 public key; both compute shared secret via X25519 DH; initiator encrypts credential payload with XChaCha20-Poly1305 using HKDF-SHA256 of shared secret; payload transmitted; responder decrypts, re-encrypts each credential to its own TPM key, stores locally; both sides zeroize shared secret and ephemeral keys
Sync message format: CBOR-encoded with fields for sync protocol version, ephemeral public key, encrypted payload, nonce, and HMAC for integrity verification of the outer message
Sync conflict resolution: last-write-wins based on credential modification timestamp, with conflict log maintained for user review; user can manually resolve conflicts via dashboard

OAuth auto-refresh: when an OAUTH_PAIR credential's access token is expired or within a configurable refresh window (default 5 minutes before expiry), the system decrypts the refresh token, performs the OAuth refresh flow, encrypts the new access token, and updates the vault atomically

Dashboard indicator computation: green if service has a _locd-svc DNS record and authentication succeeded via Loc'd protocol; yellow if service has no Loc'd support and credentials are present, valid, and not flagged; red if any credential for the service is expired, flagged weak, flagged reused, or has a known compromise indicator

Credential health check engine: runs on configurable interval (default hourly), checks password entropy (flag if < 60 bits), detects cross-service reuse via constant-time comparison of credential hashes (BLAKE2b), checks OAuth token expiry, and reports results to dashboard indicator system
Password entropy estimation: use zxcvbn-style analysis to estimate effective entropy bits; flag credentials below configurable threshold (default 60 bits) as weak
All cryptographic operations MUST use well-audited libraries (libsodium or equivalent) and MUST NOT implement custom cryptographic primitives
Vault file integrity: entire vault file is authenticated with a HMAC-SHA256 tag derived from the TPM-bound key, verified on every read to detect tampering or corruption

Credential injection supports protocol-specific adapters: HTTP Basic Auth header injection, HTTP Bearer token header injection, custom header injection, request body field injection, and TLS client certificate presentation
